title: Add tests for approval UX improvements
description:

## What to implement

1. **Auto-approve logic:**

2. **Compact diff summary:**

title: Add compact diff summary for bead display
description:
## Background
When beads are presented for approval in src/tools/approve.ts, the formatBead function shows the first 3 lines of description plus file list. For returning polish rounds, users have already seen the full descriptions — they need to see what CHANGED, not the full content again.

## What to implement
Add a compact diff summary mode that activates after the first polish round (round >= 1):

1. Create a `diffBeadSnapshots` function that compares the previous snapshot (`_lastBeadSnapshot`) with current beads and produces a human-readable diff summary:
   - Added beads: list new IDs and titles
   - Removed beads: list removed IDs
   - Modified beads: show which fields changed (title, description length delta, files changed)
   - Unchanged beads: just count them
2. In the approval UI (the `beadListText` construction), when `round >= 1` AND `_lastBeadSnapshot` exists:
   - Show the compact diff summary FIRST
   - Then show the full bead list in a collapsed/abbreviated form (ID + title only, one line each)
3. For round 0 (first presentation), keep the existing detailed format

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] diffBeadSnapshots function produces accurate add/remove/modify/unchanged summaries
- [ ] Round 0 shows full bead details (existing behavior preserved)
- [ ] Round >= 1 shows diff summary followed by abbreviated bead list
- [ ] Unit tests for diffBeadSnapshots with various change scenarios
- [ ] npm run build passes
- [ ] npm test passes

title: Iterative polish loop with convergence detection
description:
Replace the single-pass refinement in orch_approve_beads with an iterative polish loop that tracks convergence and stops at steady-state.

## What to implement

### 1. Add polish tracking to OrchestratorState in src/types.ts
- polishRound: number (current round, 0-indexed)
- polishChanges: number[] (change count per round — beads added, removed, or modified)
- polishConverged: boolean (set when 0 changes for 2 consecutive rounds)

### 2. Rewrite refinement flow in src/tools/approve.ts
Current flow: user clicks '🔍 Refine' → one LLM pass → user re-triggers orch_approve_beads.
New flow:
- On entering orch_approve_beads, read state.polishRound (default 0)
- If phase is refining_beads, increment polishRound, compute change delta (see #4), display results
- UI options per round:
  - 'Polish again (round N)' — re-enter refinement
  - '▶️ Start implementing' — proceed to execution
  - '❌ Reject' — stop
- When polishChanges has two consecutive 0s, add label to 'Start implementing': '(steady-state reached ✅)'
- Cap at 12 rounds — after that, show 'Max rounds reached. Implement or reject.'
- Remove the inner while(polishing) loop — it's replaced by the state machine

### 3. Update beadRefinementPrompt() in src/prompts.ts
- Accept roundNumber and priorChanges parameters:
  beadRefinementPrompt(roundNumber?: number, priorChanges?: number[]): string
- Include in output: 'This is polish round {N}.'
- If priorChanges provided and non-empty, include: 'Prior rounds: Round 1: {n} changes, Round 2: {n} changes, ...'
- DO NOT add ultrathink or self-containment here — those are in the separate prompt-hardening bead (x5c)

### 4. Count changes between rounds
- Before returning the refinement prompt, snapshot open beads via readBeads(): store as Map<id, {title, descHash}> where descHash = simple length + first 50 chars (cheap, no crypto needed)
- On next entry to orch_approve_beads in refining_beads phase: snapshot again, diff against stored snapshot
- Count: beads added + beads removed + beads with changed descHash
- Store in state.polishChanges[roundNumber]
- Store snapshot in a module-level variable (not in OrchestratorState — it's transient)

## Why it matters
The beads-workflow skill's core principle is 'check your beads N times, implement once' with 6-9 rounds. Our single-pass refinement is the biggest gap. Convergence detection prevents both under-polishing and infinite loops.

## Key design decision
The polish loop is driven by the LLM re-calling orch_approve_beads after each pass (same as current flow), NOT by an internal while loop. The state machine (phase + polishRound) tracks where we are. This means:
- Each round gets a full LLM turn with fresh context
- The user sees progress between rounds
- Session restore works (polishRound is persisted)

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] polishRound, polishChanges, polishConverged added to OrchestratorState
- [ ] beadRefinementPrompt accepts round/changes params and renders them
- [ ] Change count computed per round by diffing bead snapshots
- [ ] Steady-state detected (0 changes for 2 consecutive rounds)
- [ ] Max rounds cap (12)
- [ ] UI shows round info and change counts
- [ ] No overlap with prompt-hardening bead (x5c) — no ultrathink/self-containment here
- [ ] npm run build succeeds
- [ ] npm test passes

title: Integrate bv (beads-viewer) for graph health and parallel planning
description:
Add bv (beads-viewer) integration for graph health analysis and smarter next-bead selection. bv provides graph-theoretic analysis (PageRank, betweenness, k-core, critical path, slack) that our manual br dep cycles + br ready cannot replicate.

## What to implement

### 1. Add bv helpers to src/beads.ts
- detectBv(): boolean — check if bv CLI is available via `which bv`, cache result (same pattern as detectCass in memory.ts)
- bvInsights(pi, cwd): run `bv --robot-insights`, parse JSON, return typed BvInsights with:
  - Bottlenecks: { ID: string, Value: number }[]
  - Cycles: null | array
  - Orphans: string[]
  - Articulation: string[] (single points of failure)
  - Slack: { ID: string, Value: number }[]
- bvNext(pi, cwd): run `bv --robot-next`, parse JSON, return { id, title, score, reasons, unblocks } or null if no actionable items

### 2. Enhance validateBeads() in src/beads.ts
- When bv is available, call bvInsights() and use its Cycles/Orphans instead of manual detection
- Add warnings (non-blocking) for:
  - Bottleneck beads (betweenness > 5): 'bead X is a bottleneck — consider splitting'
  - Articulation points: 'bead X is a single point of failure in the dep graph'
- Return new field: warnings: string[]
- When bv unavailable, fall back to current manual cycle/orphan detection

### 3. Use bv --robot-next in src/tools/review.ts for next-bead selection
- In the 'user said looks good' branch (line ~240), when determining the next bead:
  - If bv available: call bvNext() — it considers unblock potential and graph centrality
  - If bv unavailable OR bvNext returns null: fall back to current readyBeads()[0]
- This only affects the SINGLE next bead case. The parallel case (ready.length > 1) stays unchanged — it already handles parallelism correctly.

### 4. Types
- Add BvInsights and BvNextPick interfaces to src/types.ts or inline in beads.ts

### 5. Tests
- Mock bv CLI output in src/beads.test.ts for: bvInsights with bottlenecks/cycles, bvNext with a pick, bvNext with no actionable items, bv unavailable fallback

## Key design decision: NOT using bv --robot-plan for parallel groups
After examining the actual bv --robot-plan output, its 'tracks' contain sequential items within each track (a track is a dependency chain). The orchestrator already handles parallelism correctly: it calls br ready to get all unblocked beads and launches them in parallel. bv --robot-plan would require extracting only the first item from each track, which is exactly what br ready already does. The real value of bv is in --robot-insights (validation) and --robot-next (smart ordering), not --robot-plan.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] detectBv() works with caching
- [ ] bvInsights() returns typed bottleneck/cycle/orphan/articulation data
- [ ] bvNext() returns the optimal next bead or null
- [ ] validateBeads() uses bv insights when available, with warning strings
- [ ] review.ts uses bvNext for single-next-bead selection
- [ ] Graceful fallback when bv unavailable — current behavior preserved
- [ ] Tests with mocked bv CLI output
- [ ] npm run build succeeds
- [ ] npm test passes

title: B1: bv-first bead ordering in parallel launch
description:
## Background
When `orch_approve_beads` returns the parallel launch JSON, beads are ordered by `br ready --json` output (arbitrary). `bvNext()` IS called in review.ts (L230) for subsequent launches but only reorders the array — since all beads launch in parallel, ordering doesn't affect which starts first.

## The Real Value
bv's value isn't ordering within a parallel batch (they all run simultaneously). It's:
1. **Prioritizing which bead to assign to sequential launch** (single ready bead case)
2. **Filtering**: if bv identifies a bead as a critical bottleneck, it should be implemented FIRST (possibly sequentially) before launching the rest in parallel
3. **Surfacing insights**: telling the LLM which beads are highest-impact so it can make informed launch decisions

## What To Do
1. Add `bvPlan(pi, cwd): Promise<string | null>` to `src/beads.ts` — wraps `bv --robot-plan` which returns parallel execution tracks with ordering
2. In `src/tools/approve.ts`, when building the parallel launch block:
   - Call `bvPlan()` or `bvInsights()` 
   - If bv identifies a critical bottleneck bead (high PageRank + high betweenness), suggest launching it FIRST (sequential) before the rest go parallel
   - Include bv priority info in the launch instructions text so the LLM understands the reasoning
3. In `src/tools/review.ts`, the existing bvNext() call is fine — keep it
4. If bv unavailable, fall back silently to current behavior

## Key Codebase Facts
- `bvNext(pi, cwd)` at beads.ts L48: runs `bv --robot-next`, returns `{id}` or null
- `bvInsights(pi, cwd)` at beads.ts L31: runs `bv --robot-insights`, returns string or null
- `detectBv(pi, cwd)` at beads.ts: checks bv availability
- review.ts L230: bvNext already used for ordering (cosmetic for parallel)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `bvPlan()` exported from `src/beads.ts`
- [ ] Approve flow uses bv to identify critical bottleneck beads
- [ ] Critical bottlenecks suggested for sequential-first launch
- [ ] bv insights included in launch instruction text
- [ ] Falls back gracefully when bv unavailable
- [ ] `npm run build` and `npm test` pass

title: Add convergence-based auto-approve logic
description:
## Background
src/tools/approve.ts has a polish loop that tracks changes between rounds and computes convergence scores. Currently, even when convergence is high (>=0.90) or steady-state is reached (0 changes for 2 rounds), the user must still manually click 'Start implementing'. This creates unnecessary friction.

## What to implement
Add an auto-approve mode that automatically proceeds to implementation when convergence criteria are met, with a brief notification to the user. Specifically:

1. Add a new config option `autoApproveOnConvergence` (default: true) to OrchestratorState or a config interface
2. When `polishConverged === true` OR `convergenceScore >= 0.90`, AND `autoApproveOnConvergence` is enabled:
   - Show a 3-second countdown notification: 'Beads converged — auto-approving in 3s (press any key to review)'
   - If no interruption, proceed directly to the implementation phase (reuse existing approval logic)
   - If interrupted, show the normal approval UI
3. The auto-approve should still run the quality gate check before proceeding

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Quality gate still runs before auto-approve proceeds
- [ ] User can interrupt/override auto-approve
- [ ] Existing manual approval flow unchanged when convergence not met

title: Bead quality scoring on WHAT/WHY/HOW criteria
description:
## Background
The flywheel emphasizes that beads should be self-contained executable memory. Good beads answer: WHAT (implementation details), WHY (rationale, intent, context), HOW (verification, tests, acceptance). The orchestrator should score beads on these axes during polishing to identify weak ones.

## What to Implement
1. Add a bead quality scorer that evaluates each bead on 3 axes (1-5 scale):
   - WHAT: Does the description specify concrete implementation details?
   - WHY: Does it explain rationale, context, and intent?
   - HOW: Does it include acceptance criteria, test expectations, verification?
2. Integrate into the polish flow: highlight beads scoring < 3 on any axis
3. Auto-suggest improvements for low-scoring beads
4. Show aggregate quality score during `orch_approve_beads`

## Acceptance Criteria

title: Swarm marching orders with staggered launch and thundering herd prevention
description:
## Background
The flywheel describes the 'thundering herd' problem: when all agents start simultaneously, they pile onto the same frontier. The fix: stagger starts by 30+ seconds, ensure agents mark beads in-progress quickly. Currently the orchestrator's parallel execution via WorktreePool launches all agents simultaneously.

## What to Implement
1. Add staggered launch to parallel_subagents calls:
   - Configurable stagger delay (default 30s)
   - Each agent starts after the previous one has had time to claim work
2. Add the flywheel's canonical 'Swarm Marching Orders' prompt as a template:
   - Read AGENTS.md, register with agent-mail, query bv, claim beads, coordinate
3. Ensure agents mark beads as in_progress immediately upon claiming
4. Add anti-thundering-herd check: if 2+ agents claim the same bead, detect and reassign

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Staggered launch with configurable delay
- [ ] `swarmMarchingOrders()` prompt template matching flywheel canonical version
- [ ] Agents mark beads in_progress on claim
- [ ] Duplicate claim detection and resolution
- [ ] Works with both worktree and same-dir parallel execution

title: Blunder hunt with overshoot mismatch technique
description:
## Background
The flywheel describes a powerful technique: 'Models tend to stop looking for problems after finding ~20-25 issues. If you tell them to find all problems, they stop early. The solution: lie to them and give them a huge number.' This is the 'overshoot mismatch hunt' pattern.

## What to Implement
Add a blunder hunt mode to the bead polishing flow in `orch_approve_beads`:

1. New prompt template `blunderHuntInstructions()` that uses the overshoot technique:
   'I am positive that you missed or screwed up at least 80 elements...'
2. Can be triggered as a refinement pass type (alongside the existing polish)
3. Runs 5x consecutively (the flywheel recommends repeating the EXACT same critique prompt 5 times)
4. Each pass's findings are accumulated and applied
5. Dedup findings across passes

## Acceptance Criteria

title: Default to fresh-agent refinement after first polish round
description:
## Problem
The bead refinement loop defaults to same-agent polish (`🔍 Polish again`) which suffers from anchoring — the same LLM reviews beads it just created and tends to rubber-stamp them. The codebase already acknowledges this: the fresh-agent option exists with comments like 'Fresh conversations prevent the model from anchoring on its own prior output.' But it's buried as an optional menu item.

## What to implement

### 1. Reorder options in approve.ts after round 1
In src/tools/approve.ts, the options array is built at ~line 178. After the first polish round (round >= 1):
- Move `🧠 Fresh-agent refinement` to be the FIRST option (above normal polish)
- Rename normal polish to `🔍 Same-agent polish (may anchor)` to make the tradeoff visible
- Keep other options (blunder hunt, dedup, cross-model) in their current positions

### 2. Add a convergence-aware auto-suggestion
When convergence score is below 0.5 (significant changes still happening), append a note to the select prompt: '💡 Tip: Fresh-agent refinement recommended — reduces anchoring bias.'

### 3. No behavioral changes to fresh-agent or polish logic
This is purely a UX/ordering change. The underlying fresh-agent and polish flows remain identical.

## Design notes
- This is a conservative change: we're reordering menu items and adding a label, not forcing behavior
- Users can still choose same-agent polish — it's just no longer the default position
- The convergence tip helps users make informed choices without being prescriptive

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] After round >= 1, fresh-agent refinement is the first option in the select menu
- [ ] Normal polish is relabeled to indicate anchoring risk
- [ ] Convergence tip appears when score < 0.5
- [ ] Menu order is unchanged for round 0 (first polish is always same-agent, which is fine)
- [ ] npm run build passes
- [ ] npm test passes

title: Research and reimagine workflow for major features
description:
## Background
The flywheel describes a powerful pattern for adding major features to existing projects: study an external project that already solves a related problem, then reimagine its strongest ideas through the lens of your own project. This follows a specific 9-step sequence: investigate, deepen, invert, blunder-hunt, architectural innovation, close gaps, self-contain, multi-model feedback, apply+de-slopify.

## What to Implement
1. Add a new discovery mode: '🔬 Research & Reimagine' in `orch_select`
2. When selected, user provides a URL/repo to study
3. Spawns a sub-agent that:
   a. Clones/scrapes the external project
   b. Investigates architecture and key ideas
   c. Produces a proposal document reimagining ideas for the current project
4. Runs iterative deepening + inversion analysis
5. Blunder hunt (5x) on the proposal
6. Multi-model feedback if deep-plan models available
7. Converts final proposal to beads

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] New discovery mode available in select flow
- [ ] User can provide external repo URL or documentation URL
- [ ] Sub-agent investigates external project and produces proposal
- [ ] Iterative deepening prompt applied
- [ ] Inversion analysis prompt applied (what can we do they cannot?)
- [ ] Blunder hunt runs on proposal
- [ ] Proposal converted to beads via standard flow
- [ ] `researchReimaginInstructions()` prompt templates

description:
## Background

title: D4: Document agent fungibility framing in architecture
description:
## Background
pi-orchestrator's hit-me review agents have specialized roles (fresh-eyes, polish, ergonomics, reality-check, random-exploration). The flywheel's kernel invariant #6 says swarm agents must be fungible. However, pi-orchestrator's 'specialization' is actually prompt diversity — any agent can run any prompt, agents are spawned fresh each time, and there's no persistent identity.

## What To Do
1. Update `docs/architecture.md` Section 4 (Review) to clarify:
   - Review agents use different prompts but are fully interchangeable
   - Any model can run any review prompt
   - Agents are ephemeral — no persistent identity or specialization
   - This satisfies the spirit of fungibility (invariant #6) while getting better reviews through prompt diversity
2. Add a note about the deliberate design choice and how it differs from the flywheel's anti-pattern of 'specialist agents' (which refers to long-running agents with unique roles that become single points of failure)

## Why It Matters
Clarifies a potential confusion point for contributors and future agents reading the architecture docs.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Distinguishes 'prompt diversity' from 'specialist agents'

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# E2E validation for UX simplifications S1/S2/S3/S5
# Run: bash scripts/e2e-simplification-check.sh
set -euo pipefail

PASS=0; FAIL=0

check() {
  local desc="$1" cmd="$2"
  if eval "$cmd" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "  ✅ $desc"; ((PASS++))
  else
    echo "  ❌ $desc"; ((FAIL++))
  fi
}

  "! grep -q Standard|Creative.*think.*100|Wizard.*rubric src/tools/profile.ts"
check "discoveryInstructions has no mode param" \
  "! grep -q mode.*standard.*wizard.*creative src/prompts.ts"

check "No standalone Blunder hunt in main options" \
  "! grep -E options.push.*Blunder src/tools/approve.ts"
```

title: Epic: Replace PlanStep with beads+agent-mail as core execution substrate
description:
Fundamental architectural shift: beads (br CLI) become THE planning and execution primitive. Agent-mail handles all coordination. PlanStep type and orch_plan tool are removed entirely.

## Why
- The orchestrator currently has its own PlanStep abstraction that duplicates what beads already do (tasks, deps, status lifecycle)
- Agent-mail provides file reservations and messaging that the orchestrator reimplements with worktree hacks
- The Idea Wizard skill creates beads natively — forcing ideas through PlanStep is a lossy translation
- Beads are git-visible, crash-recoverable, and queryable via br CLI

## Architecture after migration
- Discovery (all modes) → idea generation → br create beads with deps
- Planning IS bead creation — no separate orch_plan tool
- Execution reads br ready --json for actionable beads
- Parallel groups derived from bead dep graph
- orch_review works with bead IDs, br close on pass
- Agent-mail threads scoped to bead IDs for review trails
- br sync --flush-only at every state transition

## What gets removed
- PlanStep type, Plan type
- orch_plan tool entirely  
- analyzeParallelGroups() (replaced by br dep graph)
- createBeadsFromPlan() (beads are created directly, not derived from PlanSteps)

Add new phases to OrchestratorPhase:
- 'creating_beads' — LLM is calling br create

title: Remove orch_plan tool, add bead approval + Phase 6 refinement + execution launch
description:
This replaces the entire orch_plan registerTool block. The new flow:

1. BEAD APPROVAL: After the LLM creates beads (phase creating_beads), the orchestrator reads br list --json and presents them to the user. UI options:
   - 'Start implementing' → proceed to execution
   - 'Refine beads' → Phase 6 refinement (interactive)
   - 'Reject' → stop

2. PHASE 6 REFINEMENT (interactive): Emit beadRefinementPrompt() telling the LLM to review beads via br list, refine with br update, check with bv. After each pass, re-present the approval UI. User triggers each pass manually (not automated).

3. EXECUTION LAUNCH: On approval, read br ready --json for first batch of executable beads. Determine parallel groups from bead dep graph. Launch execution via parallel_subagents (keyed by bead ID, not step index).

Implementation approach: This is a NEW tool or event handler — not a modification of orch_plan (which gets deleted). Could be:
- A new orch_approve_beads tool the LLM calls after creating beads
- Or automatic: the orchestrator detects phase=creating_beads completion and triggers approval UI

The automatic approach is better — fewer tool calls, less chance of the LLM forgetting.

How to detect completion: After the LLM's response ends while phase=creating_beads, inject a before_agent_start hook or use pi.on('after_turn') to read br list --json and transition to awaiting_bead_approval.

Acceptance criteria:
- orch_plan registerTool block completely removed
- Bead approval UI shows br list output with deps
- Phase 6 refinement is interactive (user-triggered passes)
- On approval, br ready --json determines first executable batch
- Execution launch works with bead IDs (parallel_subagents keyed by bead ID)
- npm run build passes

- beadDeps(pi, cwd, id): br dep list <id> --json → string[] (parent bead IDs)
- updateBeadStatus(pi, cwd, id, status) — already exists, keep
- syncBeads(pi, cwd) — already exists, keep
- validateBeads(pi, cwd) — already exists, keep

title: Quality checklist gate with description validation
description:
Add an automated quality gate that runs before implementation starts, combining shallow-description detection with the full beads-workflow quality checklist.

## What to implement

### 1. Add qualityCheckBeads() to src/beads.ts
Validates each open bead against these checks:
1. **Has substance**: description.length >= 100 chars (heuristic — short descriptions produce poor implementations)
2. **Has file scope**: description contains '### Files:' with at least one path
3. **Has acceptance criteria**: description contains '- [ ]' checkbox markers
4. **Not oversimplified**: description word count >= 50
5. **No cycles**: reuse existing validateBeads() cycle check
6. **Dependencies connected**: bead either has deps, is depended on, or is the sole bead (skip for single-bead plans)

Return: { passed: boolean, failures: { beadId: string, check: string, reason: string }[] }

Note: 'Preserves features' and 'Includes tests' are inherently subjective — they stay as polish-prompt questions, not automated checks.

### 2. Update validateBeads() return type in src/beads.ts
- Add shallowBeads: { id: string, reason: string }[] to the return value
- Populate during validation: flag empty descriptions, < 50 chars, missing ### Files: section

### 3. Wire quality gate into approve.ts
- When user clicks 'Start implementing', call qualityCheckBeads() first
- If failures exist, display them formatted:
  '⚠️ Quality issues found:\n- bead-123: No acceptance criteria (missing - [ ] checkboxes)\n- bead-456: Description too short (23 chars)'
- Offer choices: 'Go back to polish', 'Proceed anyway'
- If all pass, proceed normally (no extra prompt)

### 4. Show quality summary in bead list during approval
- Add a line like '✅ 5/5 beads pass quality checks' or '⚠️ 3/5 pass — 2 issues found' to the approval display

### 5. Tests
- Unit test qualityCheckBeads() with mock beads: one valid, one empty description, one missing files, one missing acceptance criteria
- Test that validateBeads returns shallowBeads

## Why it matters
The beads-workflow skill has an explicit 8-item quality checklist. Without automated validation, shallow beads slip through and produce garbage implementations requiring expensive rework.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] validateBeads() returns shallowBeads alongside cycles/orphans

title: Iterative plan refinement loop with fresh-context rounds
description:
## Background
The Agent Flywheel methodology emphasizes spending 85% of time on planning. A critical part is iterative refinement: paste the plan into a FRESH conversation 4-5 times, each round finding issues the previous missed. The orchestrator currently does multi-model planning (deep-plan) but lacks this iterative refinement loop.

## What to Implement
Add an iterative plan refinement phase between plan creation and bead conversion. After the initial plan (or deep plan synthesis), offer the user a refinement loop:

1. After beads are created, add a new `refine` option in `orch_approve_beads` that triggers a refinement round
2. Each refinement round spawns a fresh sub-agent (no prior context) that:
   - Reads the current beads via `br list --json`
   - Applies the flywheel's refinement prompt: 'Carefully review each bead for better architecture, missing features, robustness improvements'
   - Returns git-diff style suggested changes
3. Track round count and convergence (output size shrinking, change velocity slowing)
4. After 3+ rounds, show convergence score and suggest stopping when score > 0.75

Key flywheel principle: 'Fresh conversations prevent the model from anchoring on its own prior output.'

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Refinement rounds use fresh sub-agents (no conversation history from creation)
- [ ] Each round's changes are applied via `br update`
- [ ] Convergence detection tracks: output size, change count, content similarity
- [ ] User can stop refinement at any time
- [ ] Convergence score displayed after round 3+
- [ ] Works with both standard and deep-plan modes
- [ ] Tests for convergence calculation logic

title: S3: Auto-advance gates — only prompt on peer review and ship
description:
## Overarching Goal
Reduce the tedious endgame of the orchestration workflow. After all beads are implemented and reviewed, users go through 7 sequential gates where they typically skip most of them. Auto-advance the low-risk read-only gates and only prompt for gates that need user judgment or are destructive/expensive.

## Background & Reasoning
The current guided gates flow (src/gates.ts) presents 7 gates sequentially. For each gate, the user sees:
```
Round N — [emoji] [label]
  [emoji] [label] — [desc]
  ⏭️ Skip
  ✅ Done — finish orchestration
```

Most gates get skipped. The result is 5+ skip clicks before reaching "Done".

## Design Decision: Sequential Auto-Advance (NOT Batching)
We considered batching all auto-run gates into a single response, but this has problems:
1. The LLM must execute ALL instructions before returning — if it gets confused mid-batch, there is no checkpoint
2. Loss of `currentGateIndex` granularity — cant resume between auto-gates on crash
3. Each gate produces unique context (e.g., self-review findings feed into test coverage decisions)

Instead, we keep the sequential structure but auto-advance without prompting for low-risk gates. Each auto-gate returns its instructions, the LLM executes them, calls `orch_review` with `__gates__`, and the NEXT gate fires automatically (auto or prompted). This preserves checkpointing and context flow.

## Gate Classification

### Auto-advance gates (return instructions immediately, no user prompt):
- **🔍 Fresh self-review** — read-only, non-destructive, always useful
- **🧪 Test coverage** — read/write but non-destructive (creates tests), always useful
- **✏️ De-slopify** — edit-only, non-destructive, auto-skips if no doc files (existing behavior preserved)

### User-prompted gates (need judgment or are expensive/destructive):
- **👥 Peer review** — spawns 4 parallel agents, uses significant compute; user should opt in
- **📦 Commit** — KEPT (not removed). Some users want the orchestrator to handle commit strategy; removing it loses functionality. However, label it clearly: "Commit — group changes into logical commits"
- **🚀 Ship it** — destructive (tags, releases, deploys); must get user permission
- **🛬 Landing checklist** — KEPT as prompted. This asks the user to verify the session is resumable — a judgment call, not something to auto-run.

### Gate order (same as current, no reordering):
0. 🔍 Fresh self-review (auto)
1. 👥 Peer review (prompt)
2. 🧪 Test coverage (auto)
3. ✏️ De-slopify (auto)
4. 📦 Commit (prompt)
5. 🚀 Ship it (prompt)
6. 🛬 Landing checklist (prompt)

## Implementation Changes in src/gates.ts

### Add gate metadata:
```typescript
const gates = [
  { emoji: "🔍", label: "Fresh self-review", desc: "read all new code with fresh eyes", auto: true },
  { emoji: "👥", label: "Peer review", desc: "parallel agents review each others work", auto: false },
  { emoji: "🧪", label: "Test coverage", desc: "check unit tests + e2e, create tasks for gaps", auto: true },
  { emoji: "✏️", label: "De-slopify", desc: "remove AI writing patterns from docs", auto: true },
  { emoji: "📦", label: "Commit", desc: "logical groupings with detailed messages", auto: false },
  { emoji: "🚀", label: "Ship it", desc: "commit, tag, release, deploy, monitor CI", auto: false },
  { emoji: "🛬", label: "Landing checklist", desc: "verify session is resumable", auto: false },
];
```

### Modify the gate loop:
```typescript
for (let i = startGate; i < gates.length; i++) {
  const gate = gates[i];
  
  if (gate.auto) {
    // Auto-advance: return instructions immediately, no prompt
    st.currentGateIndex = i + 1;
    oc.persistState();
    // Return the gate instructions (existing code for each gate type)
    // The agent executes them and calls orch_review __gates__ to continue
    return buildGateResponse(gate, ...);
  }
  
  // User-prompted gate: show select with execute/skip/done
  const pick = await ctx.ui.select(
    `Round ${round} — ${gate.emoji} ${gate.label}`,
    [
      `${gate.emoji} ${gate.label} — ${gate.desc}`,
      "⏭️  Skip",
      "✅ Done — finish orchestration",
    ]
  );
  
  if (!pick || pick.startsWith("✅")) { chosen = "✅"; break; }
  if (pick.startsWith("⏭️")) { st.currentGateIndex = i + 1; oc.persistState(); continue; }
  st.currentGateIndex = i + 1;
  oc.persistState();
  chosen = pick;
  break;
}
```

### What changes for auto-gates:
- The `ctx.ui.select` call is skipped — the gate runs immediately
- A brief notification is shown: `ctx.ui.notify("🔍 Auto-running: Fresh self-review", "info")`  
  (Optional — could also just include "[auto]" in the response text)
- The gate still returns instructions for the LLM to execute
- The LLM still calls `orch_review __gates__` to advance to the next gate
- `currentGateIndex` still advances normally, so session resume works

### What is NOT changing (features preserved)
- ALL 7 gates still exist — none removed
- All gate content (prompts, instructions, agent configs) unchanged
- Peer review still spawns 4 parallel agents with the same config
- De-slopify still auto-skips when no doc files (existing behavior)
- Ship-it still has the full checklist (commit, push, tag, release, CI, checksums, version bump)
- Landing checklist still runs landingChecklistInstructions()
- "Done" at any prompted gate exits to completion + CASS memory save
- currentGateIndex persists across session resume
- Agent-mail threading for peer review unchanged
- The "Hit me" fallback (chosen doesnt match any gate) still works

## Tests — src/gates.test.ts (new file)

Create a new test file that tests the gate auto-advance logic:

1. **Test: auto gates return instructions without prompting** — Mock ctx.ui.select to throw if called (it shouldnt be). Call runGuidedGates with currentGateIndex=0. Verify it returns self-review instructions without calling select.

2. **Test: prompted gates call ctx.ui.select** — Set currentGateIndex to 1 (peer review). Verify ctx.ui.select IS called.

3. **Test: auto gates advance currentGateIndex** — Start at gate 0 (auto). Verify st.currentGateIndex is incremented to 1 after return.

4. **Test: skip on prompted gate advances to next** — Mock select to return "⏭️ Skip" on gate 1 (peer review). Verify it advances to gate 2 (test coverage, auto) and returns test coverage instructions.

5. **Test: "Done" on prompted gate triggers completion** — Mock select to return "✅ Done". Verify phase is set to complete and orchestratorActive is false.

6. **Test: de-slopify auto-skips when no doc files** — Provide beads with only .ts files. Verify de-slopify gate is auto-advanced without returning instructions, moving to the next gate.

7. **Test: gate order is preserved** — Verify the gates array has the correct order and auto flags.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Auto gates (self-review, test coverage, de-slopify) return instructions without user prompt
- [ ] Prompted gates (peer review, commit, ship, landing) show select with execute/skip/done
- [ ] All 7 gates preserved — NO gates removed
- [ ] Auto gates still return instructions for the LLM to execute (not silent)
- [ ] currentGateIndex correctly advances for both auto and prompted gates
- [ ] Session resume from any gate index works correctly
- [ ] De-slopify still auto-skips when no doc files
- [ ] "Done" exits to completion + CASS memory save
- [ ] Tests verify auto vs prompted behavior, index advancement, and skip/done handling
- [ ] npm run build passes
- [ ] npm test passes

title: D1: Wire up staggered agent launch
description:
## Background
`SWARM_STAGGER_DELAY_MS = 30_000` exists in `src/prompts.ts` line 840 and is tested in `src/flywheel.test.ts`, but has ZERO runtime consumers. All parallel agents launch simultaneously.

## Key Codebase Fact
`orch_approve_beads` does NOT launch agents directly in code. It returns a JSON block (`parallel_subagents` config) to the LLM, which then calls the `parallel_subagents` tool. The orchestrator cannot insert sleeps between agent launches because it doesn't control the launch — the LLM does.

## What To Do
1. In `src/tools/approve.ts`, when constructing the parallel launch JSON for N > 2 agents:
   - Instead of returning a single `parallel_subagents` block, return N sequential `subagent` instructions with explicit stagger notes:
     'Launch agents ONE AT A TIME with 30-second gaps. Call `subagent` for each, waiting 30s between calls.'
   - Include the agent configs as individual blocks, not an array
2. For N ≤ 2: keep the current `parallel_subagents` block (no stagger needed)
3. Same pattern in `src/tools/review.ts` when launching next-batch parallel agents
4. Alternative simpler approach: add a prominent instruction in the returned text:
   '⏱️ STAGGER: Launch each agent 30 seconds apart to prevent thundering herd.'
   and trust the LLM to follow it when calling parallel_subagents sequentially

## Recommended Approach
Option 2 (simpler): add the stagger instruction to the returned text. The LLM already follows instructions from tool returns reliably. This is a 2-line change.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Stagger instruction included in parallel launch output for N > 2 agents
- [ ] `SWARM_STAGGER_DELAY_MS` referenced in the instruction
- [ ] No stagger for N ≤ 2
- [ ] `npm run build` and `npm test` pass

title: S5: Skip goal refinement for system-generated ideas
description:
## Overarching Goal
Reduce unnecessary friction in the orchestrator by not offering to "refine" goals that are already well-defined. When the user picks an idea from discovery, the idea already has a title, description, rationale, and scoring — it does not need a refinement questionnaire. Refinement should only be offered for custom goals where the user typed freeform text that may be vague.

## Background & Reasoning
Currently in src/tools/select.ts, after the user picks a system-generated idea, they see:
```
🎯 Selected: <idea title>
Would you like to refine this idea?
  ▶️  Continue — use as-is
  🎯 Refine — answer clarifying questions to sharpen the goal
```

This is an unnecessary decision point because:
1. The idea was generated with full repo context, scoring, and rationale — it IS refined
2. Goal refinement runs an LLM call to generate questions, then presents a TUI questionnaire — significant latency for marginal value
3. The refinement can actually DEGRADE the goal by over-narrowing it (the LLM-generated questions may not be relevant)
4. Users almost always pick "Continue — use as-is"

For custom goals (user typed freeform text), refinement remains valuable because the text may be vague.

## What to Change

### src/tools/select.ts (~lines 115-140)
In the `else` branch (user selected a system-generated idea):

**Before:**
```typescript
goal = `${idea.title}: ${idea.description}`;
const refineChoice = await ctx.ui.select(...);
if (refineChoice?.startsWith("🎯")) {
  const refinement = await runGoalRefinement(...);
  goal = refinement.enrichedGoal;
  refinementUsed = !refinement.skipped;
  if (refinementUsed) {
    oc.state.constraints = extractConstraints(refinement.answers);
  }
}
```

**After:**
```typescript
goal = `${idea.title}: ${idea.description}`;
// System-generated ideas are already well-defined (title + description + rationale + scoring).
// Skip refinement questionnaire — it adds latency without proportional value.
// Refinement is still offered for custom goals (the "✏️ Enter a custom goal" path above).
```

Remove the refineChoice select dialog and the runGoalRefinement call for this branch only. The custom goal branch (lines ~85-101) keeps its refinement call unchanged.

### Imports
Keep `runGoalRefinement` and `extractConstraints` imports — they are still used by the custom goal path (line ~97).

### Constraints handling
After removing refinement for system ideas, `refinementUsed` will always be `false` for this branch. The constraints input dialog (line ~148) will always fire, which is correct — the user should still be asked about constraints even without refinement.

### What is NOT changing (features preserved)
- Custom goal path ("✏️ Enter a custom goal" in select.ts) still offers refinement
- "I know what I want" path in profile.ts still offers refinement
- Goal refinement TUI widget still exists and works
- extractConstraints still works
- All goal-refinement.ts code stays — nothing deleted from that module

## Tests

### New test file: src/tools/select.test.ts
Since select.ts has no tests at all, create basic tests:

1. **Test: system idea selection skips refinement** — Mock ctx.ui.select to return an idea option, verify that runGoalRefinement is NOT called (or verify that no "Refine" select dialog appears). This can be tested by checking that the tool returns a goal matching `${idea.title}: ${idea.description}` without any refinement enrichment.

2. **Test: custom goal path still calls refinement** — Mock ctx.ui.select to return "✏️ Enter a custom goal", mock ctx.ui.input to return a goal string, verify that runGoalRefinement IS called.

3. **Test: tier header selection stops orchestration** — Mock ctx.ui.select to return "── Top Picks ──", verify orchestration stops with selected=false.

4. **Test: constraints input appears after system idea** — Verify ctx.ui.input is called for constraints after selecting a system idea.

Note: Testing the tool requires mocking OrchestratorContext (oc), ExtensionContext (ctx), and the UI methods. Follow the existing pattern from src/tools/approve.test.ts for how to set up mocks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Selecting a system-generated idea goes directly to constraints input (no "refine?" dialog)
- [ ] Custom goal path ("✏️ Enter a custom goal") still offers refinement
- [ ] "I know what I want" path in profile.ts still offers refinement
- [ ] Constraints input still appears after selecting a system idea
- [ ] Tests verify system idea path skips refinement
- [ ] Tests verify custom goal path still uses refinement
- [ ] npm run build passes
- [ ] npm test passes

title: Add parallel file-conflict quality gate
description:
## Problem
When multiple beads are independent (all returned by `br ready`), they can list the same files in their `### Files:` sections. Currently:
- qualityCheckBeads() in src/beads.ts does NOT check for file overlaps
- approve.ts does detect overlaps at execution time via `allDisjoint` (line ~430), but only uses it to toggle `canSameDir` mode — it never warns the user or blocks approval
- This means overlapping beads pass all quality checks and reach execution with silent conflict risk

## What to implement

### 1. Add file-overlap check to qualityCheckBeads() in src/beads.ts
- After existing checks, compute ready beads using readyBeads()
- For each pair of ready beads, check if their extractArtifacts() sets overlap
- If overlap found, add a QualityFailure with check='file-overlap' and reason listing the conflicting bead IDs and shared files
- This makes file conflicts visible during the approval quality gate, not just at execution time

### 2. Surface the conflict in approve.ts
- The existing quality gate flow (line ~385) already handles qualityResult.passed === false by showing failures and offering 'Go back to polish' / 'Proceed anyway'
- No changes needed in approve.ts — the new check will automatically surface through the existing quality gate UI
- Verify the flow works end-to-end: overlapping beads → quality gate fails → user sees file-overlap warning

### 3. Add tests in src/beads.test.ts
- Test: two ready beads with disjoint files → no file-overlap failure
- Test: two ready beads sharing a file → file-overlap failure with correct bead IDs and file name
- Test: three beads where only 2 share files → only those 2 flagged
- Test: beads with deps (not both ready) sharing files → no failure (sequential execution is fine)

## Design notes
- Only check READY beads (not all open beads) — beads with deps between them execute sequentially and can safely share files
- Use the existing extractArtifacts() helper to get file lists from bead descriptions
- readyBeads() requires pi.exec('br ready') — pass pi and cwd through to qualityCheckBeads (it already has them)

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] qualityCheckBeads() detects file overlaps among ready beads
- [ ] QualityFailure includes check='file-overlap' with descriptive reason
- [ ] Overlapping ready beads trigger quality gate warning during approval
- [ ] Tests cover disjoint, overlapping, and dependency-ordered cases

title: Add graph health remediation actions to bead approval flow
description:
## Problem
When `bv --robot-insights` surfaces validation issues during bead approval (orphaned beads, bottlenecks, articulation points), the user sees warnings but has NO way to act on them. The approval UI only offers: Start implementing, Polish, Fresh-agent refinement, Reject. The warnings are informational dead-ends.

## Solution
Add a '🩺 Fix graph issues' option to the approval menu when validation.warnings is non-empty OR validation.orphaned is non-empty. When selected, present sub-options:

1. **Close orphaned beads** — Run `br update <id> --status closed` for each orphaned bead. These are stale beads from previous sessions that clutter the graph.
2. **Split bottleneck beads** — For beads with high betweenness (>5), trigger a focused refinement pass that asks the LLM to break the bottleneck bead into smaller beads with better parallelism.
3. **Add missing dependencies** — For articulation points, show the bead and its neighbors and let the LLM suggest redundant dependency paths to reduce single-point-of-failure risk.

After remediation, re-run validateBeads and loop back to the approval menu.

## Implementation Details

### In src/tools/approve.ts:
- After computing `validationWarning`, check if remediation is needed: `const hasGraphIssues = validation.orphaned.length > 0 || validation.warnings.length > 0`
- If hasGraphIssues, add '🩺 Fix graph issues (N warnings)' to the options array
- When selected, show a sub-menu with specific actions based on what issues exist
- For orphan cleanup: directly call `br update <id> --status closed` for each
- For bottleneck splitting: use a focused LLM refinement prompt (similar to polish flow) that specifically asks to split the bottleneck bead
- For articulation points: surface the dependency graph around the bead and suggest adding parallel paths
- After any remediation action, re-validate and re-render the approval menu

### In src/beads.ts:
- Export a new `remediateOrphans(pi, cwd, orphanIds)` helper that closes orphaned beads
- The validateBeads return type already has all needed fields

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] When orphaned beads exist, '🩺 Fix graph issues' appears in the approval menu
- [ ] When bottleneck warnings exist, '🩺 Fix graph issues' appears in the approval menu
- [ ] Selecting it shows sub-options for each issue type that has problems
- [ ] Closing orphans actually runs `br update --status closed` and re-validates
- [ ] Bottleneck splitting triggers an LLM refinement focused on the bottleneck bead
- [ ] After remediation, the approval menu refreshes with updated validation
- [ ] npm run build passes
- [ ] npm test passes
- [ ] Existing approval flow unchanged when no graph issues

description:
## Background

## Background

## Key Architecture
`orch_approve_beads` does NOT launch agents directly. It returns JSON to the LLM which calls `parallel_subagents`. The orchestrator controls behavior via: (1) the JSON config it returns, (2) the task strings (which include agentMailTaskPreamble), and (3) the system prompt.

## What To Do
1. In `src/tools/approve.ts`, when building the parallel launch block, check `oc.state.coordinationMode`:

   - `single-branch` mode:

title: S1: Remove discovery mode select — always use wizard mode
description:
## Overarching Goal
Simplify the orchestrator by removing decisions the user shouldnt have to make. The discovery mode choice (Standard / Creative / Wizard) is one such decision: users pick before seeing any ideas, dont understand the differences, and wizard mode (scored rubric with tiers) is strictly superior to the other two.

## Background & Reasoning
Currently, after `orch_profile` scans the repo, the user sees a 4-option select:
1. "📋 Standard — 3-7 practical ideas"
2. "🚀 Creative — think of 100, tell me your 7 best"
3. "🧠 Idea Wizard — structured ideation with rubric scoring"
4. "✏️  I know what I want — enter my own goal"

Options 1-3 all do the same thing — call `orch_discover` — but with slightly different prompts:
- **Standard**: asks for 3-7 ideas with basic fields (id, title, description, category, effort, impact, rationale)
- **Creative**: asks to think of 100, output best 7, includes scoring axes but different tone
- **Wizard**: asks to think of 25-30, score on 5 weighted axes, cut/rank/merge/tier, output 10-15

Wizard is a strict superset — it includes scoring (which Standard lacks), tiering (which Standard lacks), and more rigorous filtering. Creative just uses different wording but the same scoring. There is no scenario where Standard or Creative produces better results than Wizard.

## What to Change

### 1. src/tools/profile.ts (~lines 75-147)
Remove the 4-option discovery mode select. Replace with a 2-option select:
- "💡 Suggest improvements — scan and propose ideas"  
- "✏️  I know what I want — enter my own goal"

When user picks "Suggest improvements":
- Call `discoveryInstructions(profile, scanResult)` with NO mode arg (it will default to wizard behavior)
- Use a single discoveryPrompt: "NEXT: Call orch_discover with your top 5 ideas and next 5-10 honorable mentions NOW."
- Remove the `isCreative`/`isWizard`/`discoveryModeKey` logic
- Remove the 3-branch `if/else if/else` for discoveryPrompt construction

title: Cross-model bead review
description:
Add a cross-model review option during bead polishing that sends beads to an alternative model for a second opinion.

## What to implement

### 1. Add 'Cross-model review' option in approve.ts polish loop
- New UI choice: '🔀 Cross-model review' alongside 'Polish again' and 'Start implementing'
- Only show this option when polishRound >= 1 (at least one polish pass done first)

### 2. Create crossModelBeadReview() in src/bead-review.ts (new file)
Why a new file: deep-plan.ts runs agents via `pi --print --no-extensions --tools read,bash,grep,find,ls`. Bead review needs br CLI access to read beads and suggest changes. Different tool set, different file.

Implementation:
- Read all open beads via readBeads()
- Format as a review prompt: list each bead's title, description, deps, and files
- Ask: 'Review these beads for: gaps in coverage, oversimplifications, missing dependencies, unclear scope, beads that should be split or merged. Output specific suggestions as a numbered list.'
- Run via `pi --print --tools read,bash,br` with an alternative model (use the model selection from deep-plan config or default to a different provider)
- Parse response and return as structured { suggestions: string[], rawOutput: string }

### 3. Present results and optionally apply
- Display suggestions to user in the approval UI
- User chooses: 'Apply suggestions (send back to LLM)' or 'Ignore and continue'
- If applying: inject suggestions into the next polish round's beadRefinementPrompt as extra context
- Count as a polish round (increment polishRound)

### 4. Model selection
- Check if deep-plan.ts agents config exists (it has model names)
- If not, use a hardcoded fallback model that's different from the current session's model
- The point is to get a DIFFERENT model's perspective, not the same one

## Why it matters
The beads-workflow skill requires 'at least one alternative model reviewed' before beads are ready. Different models catch different blind spots — Claude might miss what Gemini catches and vice versa.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] 'Cross-model review' option appears after round 1
- [ ] crossModelBeadReview() sends beads to alternative model
- [ ] Suggestions displayed to user
- [ ] User can apply (inject into next polish) or ignore

title: Fix cross-model review parser to handle all response formats
description:
## Problem
crossModelBeadReview() in src/bead-review.ts silently discards feedback when the model responds without numbered lists. The parseSuggestions() function (line ~112) only matches `/^\s*(\d+)\.\s+(.+)/` — bullet points, prose, markdown headers, or any non-numbered format yields suggestions=[] even if the model had substantive feedback.

Worse, the catch block (line ~88) also returns suggestions=[], so model execution errors are indistinguishable from 'no suggestions.' Both cases show the user 'No specific suggestions — beads look solid' in approve.ts (line ~310).

Additionally, the prompt says 'If the beads look solid, say so briefly — don't invent problems' which encourages terse non-actionable responses.

## What to implement

### 1. Improve parseSuggestions() in src/bead-review.ts
- Add support for bullet points: `/^\s*[-*•]\s+(.+)/`
- Add support for markdown headers as section delimiters: `/^#{1,3}\s+(.+)/`
- If zero items parsed from numbered/bullet formats, fall back to splitting on paragraph breaks and treating each non-empty paragraph as a suggestion
- Add unit tests for all formats

### 2. Surface rawOutput when parsing finds nothing
- In crossModelBeadReview(), when suggestions=[] after parsing, set a new field `fallbackUsed: true` on the result
- In approve.ts cross-model handler (~line 310), when suggestions.length === 0, show the raw output to the user instead of the misleading 'beads look solid' message. Use ctx.ui.select with options: 'Looks fine, continue' / 'Send raw feedback to polish round'

### 3. Distinguish errors from empty reviews
- Add an `error?: string` field to CrossModelReviewResult
- In the catch block, populate `error` with the error message
- In approve.ts, when `reviewResult.error` exists, show a warning: 'Cross-model review failed: {error}. Try again or continue.'
- Do NOT show 'beads look solid' for errors

### 4. Strengthen the cross-model prompt
- Remove 'don't invent problems' — replace with 'Be specific. If everything looks solid, explain briefly why each bead is well-formed. Always output a numbered list.'
- Add instruction: 'Check for: parallel-ready beads that modify the same files, closure extraction feasibility, missing error handling, vague acceptance criteria'

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] parseSuggestions() handles numbered lists, bullet points, and prose paragraphs
- [ ] New tests in src/bead-review.test.ts cover: numbered list, bullet list, mixed format, prose-only, empty output, error output
- [ ] CrossModelReviewResult has `error?: string` and `fallbackUsed?: boolean` fields
- [ ] approve.ts shows raw output when suggestions=[] (not 'beads look solid')
- [ ] approve.ts shows error warning when cross-model review fails (not 'beads look solid')
- [ ] Cross-model prompt no longer says 'don't invent problems'
- [ ] npm run build passes
- [ ] npm test passes

title: De-slopification gate for documentation and user-facing text
description:
## Background
The flywheel has a dedicated de-slopification phase: removing AI writing patterns (emdash overuse, 'Let us dive in', 'At its core', 'It is worth noting', etc.). Currently the orchestrator has no de-slop gate.

## What to Implement
1. Add a de-slopification gate to the post-implementation guided gates (after peer review, before commit)
2. New prompt template `deSlopifyInstructions()` targeting:
   - Emdash overuse → semicolons, commas, sentence splits
   - 'It is not X, it is Y' formulaic contrasts
   - 'Here is why' clickbait lead-ins
   - 'Let us dive in' forced enthusiasm
   - 'At its core...' pseudo-profound openers
3. Only triggers if documentation files (*.md, README, docs/) were modified
4. Must read and revise manually (no regex)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] De-slop gate added to guided gates sequence
- [ ] `deSlopifyInstructions()` prompt template with pattern catalogue
- [ ] Only triggers when doc files were modified
- [ ] Patterns list is extensible (array in prompts.ts)
- [ ] Gate can be skipped like other gates
